user_query:
Read the product brief at input/brief.md and the feature inventory at input/features.csv. Then apply a Steve Jobs/Jony Ive approach to radically simplify the product, name it, craft confident copy, set simple pricing, and outline a keynote. Create the following files:

1) output/one_thing.txt
- One line that states the single purpose of the product in five words or fewer.
- No hedging words (e.g., "we believe," "helps," "might").

2) output/names.txt
- 6–10 candidate product names.
- Each on its own line; each name must be 1–2 words, letters only (no numbers, hyphens, or punctuation), and feel short and evocative.
- Avoid technical terms and descriptors (no "AI", "cloud", "sync", "platform").

3) output/taglines.txt
- 8–12 concise taglines; one per line; each is max 6 words.
- Lead with human benefits, not features. Avoid: AI, API, SDK, GB, machine, customizable, flexible, helps, might, we believe.
- Confident, declarative language only.

4) output/hero.md
- First line: a headline beginning with "# ", max 7 words.
- Include two sections with headings exactly: "## Old way" and "## New way".
- Under each section, 3–5 bullets that set up the contrast (bullets starting with "- ").
- No hedging or technical jargon. Benefits over features.

5) output/pricing.json
- A single, simple plan. JSON object with keys: plan_name, price (number), currency (e.g., "USD"), billing_period ("monthly" or "yearly"), and one-sentence value statement.
- No tiers/matrices.

6) output/kill_grid.csv
- A CSV with header: Axis1,Axis2,KeepOrKill,Name,Reason
- Populate at least 4 rows that map the existing features (from input/features.csv) to a 2x grid you define (e.g., Consumer/Pro x Essential/Nice-to-have).
- Mark at least two features as Kill with clear reasons; at least one as Keep.
- Use feature names that exist in input/features.csv.

7) output/presentation.md
- A five-part keynote outline using the Jobs structure with numbered sections:
  1. Set the stage
  2. Introduce the hero
  3. Rule of three (include exactly three bullets under this section)
  4. Demo (describe a simple, brave demo)
  5. One more thing
- Keep prose tight and human; no jargon.

Use the three laws (essential, human, inevitable) and the protocols (simplify, name, copy, kill, price, present). Translate technical features into human benefits. Be decisive. If a word doesn’t earn its place, remove it.
